question: Describe the configuration of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS).

nuscale_ch01_0043.title: Ultimate Heat Sink
nuscale_ch01_0043.summary: The Ultimate Heat Sink section describes a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool located below plant grade level, which serves as the final heat removal system for the NuScale reactor. In normal operations, heat is removed from the pool via a cooling system and rejected to the atmosphere, while in the event of a total loss of AC power, decay heat is passively transferred to the pool, which has sufficient water inventory to ensure continued cooling through natural circulation and boiling.

nuscale_ch01_0673.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.3 Analytical ParametersCore Burnup
nuscale_ch01_0673.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, addressing events such as decrease in feedwater temperature, increase in feedwater or steam flow, and inadvertent operation of turbine bypass or decay heat removal systems. It confirms that analytical parameters—including limiting single failure, initial power level, conservative scram, core burnup, and setpoint inaccuracies—meet regulatory requirements, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch01_0450.title: BTP 10-2, Rev 4: Design Guidelines for Avoiding Water Hammers in Steam GeneratorsTFSGD B.1 thru B.4Top-Feed Steam Generator Designs
nuscale_ch01_0450.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design does not use top-feed, preheat, or once-through steam generator designs, so the regulatory guidelines for avoiding water hammers in these systems are not applicable. Instead, NuScale uses a passive natural circulation system (DHRS) that fulfills a similar safety function without the need for auxiliary feedwater pumps or external supply headers, thereby inherently avoiding the water hammer concerns addressed by the guidelines.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions. The report references compliance with relevant standards, including RG 1.105 and the Standard Review Plan.

nuscale_ch01_0440.title: DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: Condensate and Feedwater SystemII.1 Seismic Events (GDC 2)
nuscale_ch01_0440.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria for the condensate and feedwater systems, noting compliance with requirements to prevent excessive radioactivity release and piping failures. It also highlights areas where certain criteria are not applicable due to design differences (e.g., absence of Class 2/3 components or BWR-specific requirements) and describes a design-specific exemption from GDC 34 for residual heat removal. Overall, the NuScale design generally conforms to regulatory standards, with specific justifications provided for any departures or non-applicability.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It lists the relevant diagrams and legends provided for both I&C and piping and instrumentation diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be supplied by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0062.title: Control Rod Assembly Drop and Control Rod Drive Shaft Alignment Test
nuscale_ch01_0062.summary: This section describes the testing and validation of the NuScale control rod assembly (CRA) drop and drive shaft alignment, focusing on performance, reliability, and repeatability. Tests conducted at Curtiss Wright facilities demonstrated that the remote disconnect mechanism reliably engages and disengages the CRA, and the results have informed improvements in drive rod position detection circuitry, though they do not establish the final design basis. The NPM features longer, remotely disconnectable drive shafts with multiple alignment supports to ensure proper operation.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, critical heat flux limits, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard. No exceptions or non-conformances are noted.

edges.description: Section A describes the technical configuration and operation of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B lists regulatory criteria and accident scenarios (including inadvertent DHRS operation) that the DHRS configuration must address, connecting them through the system's required performance under specified conditions.

Section A describes the configuration and function of the DHRS, while Section B explains that the DHRS fulfills a similar function to the AFW system in large PWRs but is not subject to the same design guidelines, connecting them through the functional equivalence of the DHRS to the AFW system.

Section A describes the DHRS configuration and operation, which provides the technical basis for demonstrating conformance with the regulatory criteria outlined in Section B regarding system response to operational events.

Section A discusses operational events and analytical parameters involving the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B provides design guidelines for systems (like the DHRS) to avoid water hammers, connecting them through the shared focus on DHRS configuration and operational safety.

Section A and Section B both address analytical parameters and specific criteria for events involving the Decay Heat Removal System under DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, with Section A focusing on limiting single failures and Section B on instrument spans, setpoints, and system pressure, thus providing complementary details for the same regulatory scenarios.

Section A explains that the NuScale DHRS serves a similar safety function as the Auxiliary Feedwater System in large PWRs, while Section B provides the regulatory criteria and specific system requirements (such as instrument spans and setpoints) that the DHRS configuration must meet.

Section B provides a detailed configuration and operational description of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which is the specific system referenced in the analysis question, while Section A does not address the DHRS configuration but instead discusses related systems and regulatory criteria.

Section A describes the design and regulatory conformance of the condensate and feedwater systems, which are functionally related to the DHRS configuration discussed in Section B, where the DHRS operation is analyzed under transient conditions involving feedwater and steam systems.

Section B notes that the NuScale DHRS fulfills a similar function as the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system in large PWRs, which is referenced in Section A's discussion of feedwater and condensate systems, thus connecting the DHRS configuration to the regulatory context of Section A.

Section A provides detailed diagrams and figures that visually clarify the design and configuration of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B offers a general plant description outlining the functional arrangement and operational capabilities of the DHRS, with the diagrams in Section A specifying the details of the general description in Section B.

Section A provides detailed drawings and diagrams of the DHRS configuration, which serve as the technical basis for the analytical parameters and regulatory conformance assessments discussed in Section B.

Section A provides detailed diagrams and figures illustrating the physical configuration of the DHRS, while Section B discusses regulatory guidelines and the functional equivalence of the DHRS to traditional AFW systems, connecting them through the system's design intent and regulatory context.

Section A references the regulatory criteria and conformance for systems related to decay heat removal, while Section B provides the detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify the configuration described in Section A.

Section B provides a detailed configuration and operational description of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which is referenced in Section A as part of the analysis of system responses to various transients and inadvertent operations.

Section A and Section B both address different analytical aspects (system pressure and core burnup/limiting single failure) under the same set of DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4 transients, connecting them through their shared regulatory context for evaluating the DHRS.

Section A discusses operational events and criteria related to the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B provides design guidelines for systems (like the DHRS) that fulfill functions similar to the Auxiliary Feedwater System, connecting them through their shared safety function and design considerations.
